title: Chinese Stocks in Hong Kong Rebound From Edge of Bear Market
content:
(Bloomberg) -- A gauge of Chinese equities in Hong Kong trimmed losses, supported by a rotation into financial shares as investors shifted out of artificial intelligence-linked names.

Most Read from Bloomberg

Iran Says Hormuz Closed Again as Talks With US Set to Open US and Iran Make 'Progress' in Talks, Aim to Keep Hormuz Open Iranian Negotiators Said to Still Be Engaged in Talks With US DOJ Rejects Judge Request to Certify $1.8 Billion Fund Nixed Trump's Fears About Economy Undercut US Leverage in Iran Talks

The Hang Seng China Enterprises Index narrowed its decline to 0.3% from a loss of as much as 2.3%, with insurers like China Life Insurance Co. and lenders including Postal Savings Bank of China Co. among the biggest gainers. The MSCI China Index erased an earlier loss to trade little changed, while onshore benchmark CSI 300 Index closed up 2.4% to hit the highest level since Dec. 2021.

"Non-bank financials led gains, likely as some investors rotated out of technology stocks into brokers and insurers with clearer earnings visibility," said Fu Zhifeng, chief investment officer at Shanghai Chengzhou Investment Management Co. "Brokerages, supported by more tangible profit prospects, were seen as relatively resilient, helping underpin sentiment."

The HSCEI gauge was at one point on the brink of entering a technical bear market, weighed by weak consumption data and investors chasing AI shares in other North Asian markets. Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. and Xiaomi Corp. remained among the biggest drags on the gauge.

Investor caution persisted as a series of data showed Chinese consumers remain reluctant to spend, with focus on ongoing US‑Iran peace talks. The heavy weighting of internet and consumer tech firms such as Alibaba and Tencent Holdings Ltd., seen as lagging in the global AI rally, has contributed to the gauge's underperformance this year.

China's domestic travel during the Dragon Boat Festival holiday was flat from a year earlier, underscoring continued consumption weakness that has hindered economic recovery. Retail sales in May registered their first year-on-year fall since the reopening from Covid lockdowns in late 2022.

Shares of Alibaba and other Chinese e-commerce companies including JD.com Inc. slumped Monday, as Citigroup analysts noted that the "6.18" mid-year online shopping event drew muted demand.

"We continue seeing liquidities leaving 'old Techs' in Hong Kong including Tencent, Alibaba and Baidu to chase AI-related stocks in Japan, Korea and Taiwan," said Steven Leung, executive director at UOB Kay Hian. "I'm not expecting the trend to reverse in the near term because AI-related operating figures and demand remain strong."

--With assistance from Jing Jin.

(Updated with analyst comment seventh paragraph, latest prices)
